toolUseResult.newString:
# Write the posture-check assertion script for a pool variant to a temp
# file and echo the resulting path. The script is cloud-side bash run as
# step 1 of the per-pool build (probe or posture-only). SUCCESS terminal
# exit means reality matches the pool's stamped egress posture. Curl is
# deliberately used WITHOUT -f so HTTP 4xx/5xx responses from a reached
# server still count as "reached" — the test is reachability vs
# connection failure, not authorization. Only honest curl failures (DNS,
# connection refused, timeout) trip the assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap)
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1}"
  local -r z_path="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_${z_variant}_posture_check.sh"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com || { echo "tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com 2>/dev/null; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac

  printf '%s\n' "${z_path}"
}

# Submit a pre-composed Cloud Build against one of the depot's worker pools
# and await its terminal state.

content.content:
200	# «shebang»
201	# «copyright»
202	
203	set -euo pipefail
204	
205	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
206	
207	z«prefix»_furnish() {
208	
209	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
210	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
211	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
212	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
213	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
214	
215	  # Source all dependencies
216	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
217	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
218	
219	  # Source regime files, if any
220	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
221	
222	  z«prefix»_kindle
223	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
224	}
225	
226	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
227	
228	# eof
229	```
230	
231	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
232	
233	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
234	
235	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
236	
237	```bash
238	z«prefix»_furnish() {
239	  local z_command="${1:-}"
240	
241	  # Document environment variables
242	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
243	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
244	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
245	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
246	
247	  # Always source light dependencies
248	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
249	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
250	
251	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
252	  case "${z_command}" in
253	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
254	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
255	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
256	      zheavy_kindle
257	      ;;
258	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
259	      # These commands work with config only
260	      ;;
261	  esac
262	
263	  z«prefix»_kindle
264	}
265	```
266	
267	**Key points:**
268	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
269	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
270	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
271	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
272	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
273	
274	**Benefits:**
275	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
276	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
277	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
278	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
279	
280	### Template 3: Implementation Module
281	
282	```bash
283	# «shebang»
284	# «copyright»
285	
286	set -euo pipefail
287	
288	# Multiple inclusion detection
289	test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
290	Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1
291	
292	# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
293	# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
294	# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"
295	
296	######################################################################
297	# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)
298	
299	z«prefix»_kindle() {
300	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
301	
302	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
303	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
304	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
305	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
306	
307	  # Validate module specific environment
308	  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
309	
310	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
311	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
312	
313	  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
314	  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
315	
316	  # Kindle constants (internal)
317	  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
318	  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
319	  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
320	  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
321	
322	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
323	  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
324	
325	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
326	}
327	
328	z«prefix»_sentinel() {
329	  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
330	}
331	
332	# Helper functions follow
333	
334	######################################################################
335	# External Functions («prefix»_*)
336	
337	«prefix»_«command»() {
338	  z«prefix»_sentinel
339	
340	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
341	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
342	
343	  # Documentation block
344	  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
345	  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
346	  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
347	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
348	
349	  # Implementation
350	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
351	
352	  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
353	  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
354	  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"
355	
356	  buc_success "«success_message»"
357	}
358	```
359	
360	**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
361	
362	```bash
363	# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
364	z«prefix»_setup() {
365	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
366	}
367	
368	# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
369	z«prefix»_kindle() {
370	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
371	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
372	}
373	```
374	
375	**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
376	
377	```bash
378	# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
379	«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
380	
381	# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
382	«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
383	«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time
384	
385	# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
386	«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
387	
388	# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
389	«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
390	
391	# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
392	«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
393	
394	# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
395	«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
396	```
397	
398	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
399	
400	### Expansion Requirements
401	
402	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
403	```bash
404	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
405	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
406	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
407	```
408	
409	**Never use:**
410	```bash
411	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
412	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
413	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
414	```
415	
416	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
417	```bash
418	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
419	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
420	
421	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
422	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
423	```
424	
425	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
426	```bash
427	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
428	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
429	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
430	
431	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
432	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
433	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
434	```
435	
436	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
437	
438	### Assignment Patterns
439	
440	```bash
441	# Direct assignment
442	local z_message="Processing item"
443	
444	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
445	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
446	
447	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
448	local z_name=""
449	local z_value="default"
450	local z_result=0
451	
452	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
453	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
454	
455	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
456	local z_count="${z_input}"
457	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
458	
459	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
460	local z_token
461	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
462	
463	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
464	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
465	
466	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
467	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
468	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"
469	
470	# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
471	# ❌ local z_content
472	# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
473	
474	# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
475	local z_token
476	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
477	
478	# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
479	echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
480	complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
481	process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
482	local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
483	test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
484	```
485	
486	### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools
487	
488	```bash
489	# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
490	"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
491	"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
492	"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
493	"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
494	"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'
495	
496	# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
497	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
498	  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
499	done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"

# «shebang»
# «copyright»

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

z«prefix»_furnish() {

  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Source all dependencies
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"

  # Source regime files, if any
  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"

  z«prefix»_kindle
  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
}

buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"

# eof
```

### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)

The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.

**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.

```bash
z«prefix»_furnish() {
  local z_command="${1:-}"

  # Document environment variables
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Always source light dependencies
  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"

  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
  case "${z_command}" in
    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
      zheavy_kindle
      ;;
    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
      # These commands work with config only
      ;;
  esac

  z«prefix»_kindle
}
```

**Key points:**
- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies

**Benefits:**
- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure

### Template 3: Implementation Module

```bash
# «shebang»
# «copyright»

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1

# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)

z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps

  # Validate module specific environment
  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present

  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"

  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"

  # Kindle constants (internal)
  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use

  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"

  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}

z«prefix»_sentinel() {
  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
}

# Helper functions follow

######################################################################
# External Functions («prefix»_*)

«prefix»_«command»() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  local z_param1="${1:-}"
  local z_param2="${2:-}"

  # Documentation block
  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Implementation
  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"

  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"

  buc_success "«success_message»"
}
```

**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**

```bash
# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
z«prefix»_setup() {
  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
}

# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**

```bash
# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed

# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time

# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required

# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle

# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle

# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
```

## Variable Handling (General Rules)

### Expansion Requirements

**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
```bash
"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
```

**Never use:**
```bash
$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
```

**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
```bash
# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory

# ✅ Use $HOME instead
"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
```

**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
```bash
# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix

# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
```

When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.

### Assignment Patterns

```bash
# Direct assignment
local z_message="Processing item"

# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!

# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
local z_name=""
local z_value="default"
local z_result=0

# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!

# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
local z_count="${z_input}"
test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"

# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!

# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"

# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
# ❌ local z_content
# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity

# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
```

### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools

```bash
# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'

# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"

content.content:
500	
501	# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
502	z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
503	```
504	
505	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
506	
507	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
508	
509	```bash
510	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
511	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
512	
513	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
514	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
515	
516	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
517	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
518	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
519	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
520	
521	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
522	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
523	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
524	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
525	
526	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
527	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
528	```
529	
530	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
531	
532	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
533	
534	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
535	
536	```bash
537	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
538	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
539	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
540	
541	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
542	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
543	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
544	```
545	
546	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
547	
548	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
549	
550	```bash
551	# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
552	Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"
553	
554	# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
555	local z_index=0
556	while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
557	  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
558	  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
559	
560	  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
561	    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
562	    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
563	
564	  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
565	done
566	```
567	
568	### String Usage
569	
570	```bash
571	# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
572	jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
573	grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
574	
575	# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
576	```
577	
578	### Readonly Patterns
579	
580	Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:
581	
582	#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables
583	
584	Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.
585	
586	```bash
587	# ✅ Default: readonly locals
588	local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
589	local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
590	local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
591	local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
592	```
593	
594	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
595	
596	```bash
597	# Exception 1: Loop counters
598	local z_index=0
599	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
600	
601	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
602	local z_manifest_file=""
603	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
604	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
605	  ...
606	done
607	
608	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
609	local z_token
610	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
611	
612	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
613	local z_any_failed=0
614	local z_current_group=""
615	```
616	
617	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
618	
619	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
620	
621	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
622	
623	```bash
624	z«prefix»_kindle() {
625	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
626	
627	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
628	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
629	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
630	
631	  # Computed from already-set values
632	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
633	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
634	
635	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
636	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
637	
638	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
639	}
640	```
641	
642	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
643	
644	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
645	
646	#### Mutable Kindle State
647	
648	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
649	
650	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
651	
652	```bash
653	z«prefix»_kindle() {
654	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
655	
656	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
657	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
658	
659	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
660	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
661	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
662	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
663	
664	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
665	}
666	```
667	
668	**Rules**:
669	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
670	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
671	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
672	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
673	
674	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
675	
676	```bash
677	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
678	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
679	  z«prefix»_sentinel
680	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
681	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
682	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
683	}
684	```
685	
686	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
687	```bash
688	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
689	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
690	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
691	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
692	```
693	
694	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
695	
696	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
697	
698	```bash
699	z«prefix»_enforce() {
700	  z«prefix»_sentinel
701	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
702	  # ... custom format checks ...
703	}
704	
705	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
706	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
707	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
708	
709	# Derived state built from validated values:
710	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
711	```
712	
713	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
714	
715	## Special Function Definitions
716	
717	### Special Function Patterns
718	
719	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
720	
721	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
722	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
723	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
724	| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
725	| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
726	| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
727	| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
728	| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
729	| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |
730	
731	---
732	
733	#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
734	
735	**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
736	
737	Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
738	
739	```bash
740	z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
741	  z«prefix»_sentinel
742	
743	  # Process and validate internally
744	  local z_result
745	  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
746	
747	  # Validate without buc_die
748	  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
749	
750	  # Single output at end
751	  echo "${z_result}"
752	}
753	
754	# Caller uses two-line pattern
755	local z_value
756	z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
757	```
758	
759	**Use capture functions for:**
760	- Secrets that must never touch disk
761	- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
762	- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
763	
764	**Contract:**
765	- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
766	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
767	- No side effects beyond the returned value
768	- May source credential files when necessary for security
769	- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
770	- Never writes secrets to disk
771	- Never writes to stderr
772	
773	#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
774	
775	Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
776	
777	```bash
778	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
779	  z«prefix»_sentinel
780	
781	  local z_file="${1:-}"
782	
783	  # Return status only - no output, no dies
784	  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
785	  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1

file.content:
# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
```

### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution

All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!

# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!

# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"

# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"

# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
```

**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.

### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress

Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"

# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
```

**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.

**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:

```bash
# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"

# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
local z_index=0
while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"

  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"

  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
done
```

### String Usage

```bash
# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"

# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
```

### Readonly Patterns

Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:

#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables

Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.

```bash
# ✅ Default: readonly locals
local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
```

**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:

```bash
# Exception 1: Loop counters
local z_index=0
z_index=$((z_index + 1))

# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
local z_manifest_file=""
for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  ...
done

# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
local z_any_failed=0
local z_current_group=""
```

**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.

#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant

Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:

```bash
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3

  # Computed from already-set values
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"

  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"

  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.

**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.

#### Mutable Kindle State

Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.

**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.

```bash
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"

  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment

  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

**Rules**:
- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)

**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.

```bash
# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel
  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
}
```

**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
```bash
# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
```

#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce

For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):

```bash
z«prefix»_enforce() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel
  buv_vet «SCOPE»
  # ... custom format checks ...
}

# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE

# Derived state built from validated values:
Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
```

**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.

## Special Function Definitions

### Special Function Patterns

This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.

| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |

---

#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)

**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**

Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.

```bash
z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  # Process and validate internally
  local z_result
  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1

  # Validate without buc_die
  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1

  # Single output at end
  echo "${z_result}"
}

# Caller uses two-line pattern
local z_value
z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
```

**Use capture functions for:**
- Secrets that must never touch disk
- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
- Any computed value where clean error handling matters

**Contract:**
- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
- Never uses `buc_die` internally
- No side effects beyond the returned value
- May source credential files when necessary for security
- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
- Never writes secrets to disk
- Never writes to stderr

#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)

Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.

```bash
z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  local z_file="${1:-}"

  # Return status only - no output, no dies
  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1

  return 0
}

# Caller uses in conditionals
z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
```

#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)

**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.

**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length

input.old_string:
# Write the posture-check assertion script for a pool variant to a temp
# file and echo the resulting path. The script is cloud-side bash run as
# step 1 of the per-pool build (probe or posture-only). SUCCESS terminal
# exit means reality matches the pool's stamped egress posture. Curl is
# deliberately used WITHOUT -f so HTTP 4xx/5xx responses from a reached
# server still count as "reached" — the test is reachability vs
# connection failure, not authorization. Only honest curl failures (DNS,
# connection refused, timeout) trip the assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap)
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1}"
  local -r z_path="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_${z_variant}_posture_check.sh"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com || { echo "tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com 2>/dev/null; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac

  printf '%s\n' "${z_path}"
}
input.new_string:
# Write the posture-check assertion script for a pool variant to its
# kindle-constant path. Side-effect only — caller passes the matching
# ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_* constant to the pool-build submitter. The script
# is cloud-side bash run as step 1 of the per-pool build (probe or
# posture-only). SUCCESS terminal exit means reality matches the pool's
# stamped egress posture. Curl is deliberately used WITHOUT -f so HTTP
# 4xx/5xx responses from a reached server still count as "reached" —
# the test is reachability vs connection failure, not authorization.
# Only honest curl failures (DNS, connection refused, timeout) trip the
# assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap)
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_variant}" || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: variant required"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com || { echo "tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_TETHER}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_AIRGAP}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac
}

        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_AIRGAP}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac
}

content.subject: Extract zrbgp_pool_build_submit_await primitive

content.subject: Extract zrbgp_write_posture_check helper
content.description: Pull printf blocks at rbgp_Payor.sh:1101-1127 into helper that writes ${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_${variant}_posture_check.sh and echoes path. Variant case switch (tether|airgap).

content.subject: Rewrite posture check bash with honest curl idiom
content.description: No -f flag. Tether: example.com + storage.googleapis.com each must respond (any HTTP code). Airgap: example.com must fail to connect, storage.googleapis.com must respond. SUCCESS = posture correct.

content.subject: Refactor zrbgp_pool_probe_submit to use new primitives
content.description: Levy probe submit: composes 2-step build JSON (posture check + marker push), delegates to submit-await primitive. Keeps marker push and "Per-pool probe" labeling.

content.description: New helper composes 1-step build (posture check only), delegates to submit-await. New verb rbgp_depot_info: authenticate as Payor, derive constants, call posture submit for tether then airgap.

# Depot-info per-pool posture check. Composes a 1-step Cloud Build that
# runs the posture-check assertion against an existing worker pool and
# delegates to zrbgp_pool_build_submit_await. Distinct from the levy's
# 2-step probe: no marker push, no GAR write — purely an egress-posture
# diagnostic against a pool that has already been levied and probed.
# SUCCESS terminal = pool's egress posture matches its stamped config;
# FAILURE = drift worth investigating via the console link.
# Args: token pool_variant pool_id mason_email posture_check_file
zrbgp_pool_posture_submit() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_token="${1:-}"
  local -r z_pool_variant="${2:-}"
  local -r z_pool_id="${3:-}"
  local -r z_mason_email="${4:-}"
  local -r z_posture_check_file="${5:-}"

  test -n "${z_token}"              || buc_die "zrbgp_pool_posture_submit: token required"
  test -n "${z_pool_variant}"       || buc_die "zrbgp_pool_posture_submit: pool_variant required"
  test -n "${z_pool_id}"            || buc_die "zrbgp_pool_posture_submit: pool_id required"
  test -n "${z_mason_email}"        || buc_die "zrbgp_pool_posture_submit: mason_email required"
  test -n "${z_posture_check_file}" || buc_die "zrbgp_pool_posture_submit: posture_check_file required"

  local -r z_region="${RBRR_GCP_REGION}"
  local -r z_pool_resource="projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${z_region}/workerPools/${z_pool_id}"
  local -r z_mason_sa="projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/serviceAccounts/${z_mason_email}"

  local -r z_build_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_posture_${z_pool_variant}_build.json"
  jq -n \
    --arg     posture_image   "gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk:alpine" \
    --rawfile posture_check   "${z_posture_check_file}" \
    --arg     pool_resource   "${z_pool_resource}" \
    --arg     service_account "${z_mason_sa}" \
    '{
      steps: [
        {
          name:   $posture_image,
          id:     "assert-egress-posture",
          script: $posture_check
        }
      ],
      serviceAccount: $service_account,
      options: {
        logging: "CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY",
        pool: { name: $pool_resource }
      }
    }' > "${z_build_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to compose posture build JSON for ${z_pool_variant}"

  zrbgp_pool_build_submit_await \
    "${z_token}" "${z_pool_variant}" "${z_pool_id}" \
    "${z_build_file}" "depot_posture" "Posture check" ""
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbgp_*)

  buc_doc_brief "Run egress posture checks against the live depot's tether and airgap worker pools"

}

message.content: First lets discuss why we have kindle constants that are paths to scripts: that surprised me.  Explain?

lastPrompt: First lets discuss why we have kindle constants that are paths to scripts: that surprised me.  Explain?

content.text:
Good catch. The kindle constants here are mis-scoped — they're really transient per-invocation state, not module-level invariants.

**Why I reached for kindle constants.** BCG forbids `$()` on non-`_capture` functions, and my writer has a side effect (writes a file) which disqualifies it from being a capture (BCG line 767: "No side effects beyond the returned value"). So the writer can't return the path. I needed both the writer and the caller to agree on the path; kindle constants gave them a shared source of truth.

**Why that's wrong.** Kindle constants are for module-level state that persists across the module's lifetime: the empty-JSON scratch file, the SA email domain, infix-name constants — things that are decided once when the module wakes up and never change. The posture-check script paths aren't like that. Each `rbgp_depot_levy` or `rbgp_depot_info` invocation writes the script, hands the path to the submitter, and the file is done. It's per-invocation scratch, not module identity.

The existing kindle in this file already has the right pattern for what I should have used:
- `ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE` and `ZRBGP_EMPTY_JSON` are truly module-level (one scratch, one literal `{}` written at kindle time and reused forever)
- My posture-check paths are not in that category — they're rewritten on every verb invocation

**Cleaner factoring.** The writer takes the path as a parameter; the caller composes the path locally. One line in the verb, no module state:

```bash
local -r z_tether_path="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_tether_posture_check.sh"
local -r z_airgap_path="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_airgap_posture_check.sh"
zrbgp_write_posture_check tether "${z_tether_path}"
zrbgp_write_posture_check airgap "${z_airgap_path}"
zrbgp_pool_probe_submit "${z_token}" "tether" "${z_tether_id}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_tether_path}"
zrbgp_pool_probe_submit "${z_token}" "airgap" "${z_airgap_id}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_airgap_path}"
```

The writer's signature becomes `zrbgp_write_posture_check <variant> <path>`. Both callers (levy and depot_info) compose their own paths.

Want me to refactor to that, drop the kindle constants?

message.content: yes refactor and drop the kindle constants.  Then yes you may call the tabtarget to see if it works

}

input.old_string:
# Write the posture-check assertion script for a pool variant to its
# kindle-constant path. Side-effect only — caller passes the matching
# ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_* constant to the pool-build submitter. The script
# is cloud-side bash run as step 1 of the per-pool build (probe or
# posture-only). SUCCESS terminal exit means reality matches the pool's
# stamped egress posture. Curl is deliberately used WITHOUT -f so HTTP
# 4xx/5xx responses from a reached server still count as "reached" —
# the test is reachability vs connection failure, not authorization.
# Only honest curl failures (DNS, connection refused, timeout) trip the
# assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap)
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_variant}" || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: variant required"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com || { echo "tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_TETHER}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_AIRGAP}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac
}
input.new_string:
# Write the posture-check assertion script for a pool variant to the
# caller-provided path. Side-effect only — caller hands the same path
# to the pool-build submitter. The script is cloud-side bash run as
# step 1 of the per-pool build (probe or posture-only). SUCCESS terminal
# exit means reality matches the pool's stamped egress posture. Curl is
# deliberately used WITHOUT -f so HTTP 4xx/5xx responses from a reached
# server still count as "reached" — the test is reachability vs
# connection failure, not authorization. Only honest curl failures (DNS,
# connection refused, timeout) trip the assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap) path
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1:-}"
  local -r z_path="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_variant}" || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: variant required"
  test -n "${z_path}"    || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: path required"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com || { echo "tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac
}

toolUseResult.oldString:
# Write the posture-check assertion script for a pool variant to its
# kindle-constant path. Side-effect only — caller passes the matching
# ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_* constant to the pool-build submitter. The script
# is cloud-side bash run as step 1 of the per-pool build (probe or
# posture-only). SUCCESS terminal exit means reality matches the pool's
# stamped egress posture. Curl is deliberately used WITHOUT -f so HTTP
# 4xx/5xx responses from a reached server still count as "reached" —
# the test is reachability vs connection failure, not authorization.
# Only honest curl failures (DNS, connection refused, timeout) trip the
# assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap)
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_variant}" || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: variant required"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com || { echo "tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_TETHER}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        'if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://example.com; then echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2; exit 1; fi' \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        'curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo "airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)" >&2; exit 1; }' \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${ZRBGP_POSTURE_CHECK_AIRGAP}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac
}

201	{rbtr_mason}*:

204	.. Grant role: `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`
205	.. NOTE: Cloud Build service agent is Google-provisioned (auto-created when Cloud Build API is enabled), not a user-created service account. The `tokenCreator` role enables build steps running as {rbtr_mason} (via the build JSON's `serviceAccount` field) to generate fresh access tokens for GCP API calls — required for GAR authentication (docker login, crane push). This is distinct from `serviceAccountUser` (actAs), which the {rbtr_director} holds on {rbtr_mason} for submitting builds.
206	.. {rbbc_require}
207	IAM binding successful
208	
209	//axhos_step
210	. *Submit Per-Pool Probe Builds*:
211	.. For each worker pool variant in [`tether`, `airgap`]:
212	... {rbbc_submit}
213	link:https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.builds/create[builds.create] with:
214	.... `serviceAccount`: `projects/«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»/serviceAccounts/«MASON_EMAIL»`
215	.... `options.pool.name`: full pool resource path for the variant
216	.... `options.logging`: `CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY`
217	.... `steps[0]`: egress-posture assertion (builder: `gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk:alpine`)
218	..... tether asserts `https://example.com` reachable AND `https://storage.googleapis.com` reachable
219	..... airgap asserts `https://example.com` blocked AND `https://storage.googleapis.com` reachable
220	.... `steps[1]`: GAR write probe (builder: `gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker`) — `FROM scratch` image with a single marker file, pushed to `«REGION»-docker.pkg.dev/«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»/«GAR_REPOSITORY»/rbi_df/probe-«VARIANT»:probe`
221	... {rbbc_require}
222	submission returns HTTP 200/201 (build accepted) OR HTTP 400 (per-pool quota row absent — see below). All other codes are fatal.
223	... {rbbc_await}
224	(HTTP 200/201 only) build terminal state via `builds.get` polled every 5s, with a 480-poll (40-minute) ceiling. Any terminal state — SUCCESS / FAILURE / CANCELLED / TIMEOUT / EXPIRED / INTERNAL_ERROR — is acceptable; the probe is designed to FAILURE for egress posture assertion. The HTTP 400 branch has no build to await.
225	... {rbbc_show}
226	per-pool probe block carrying the pool ID, GAR artifact reference, and either the build ID + Cloud Build console link + terminal status (HTTP 200/201) or a quota-row-materialized notice (HTTP 400).
227	.. NOTE: Builder images are Google-hosted only (`gcr.io/...`). The airgap pool cannot reach `docker.io`; any non-Google builder would hang the build.
228	.. NOTE: On fresh levy the pool's per-resource concurrent-build quota row does not yet exist. Cloud Build's quota gate refuses the submission with HTTP 400, and that refusal IS the materialization side-effect that creates the quota row — not a failure to surface. The operator can subsequently request a quota increase against the now-existing row, then re-run levy (or any subsequent build submission) to exercise the probe end-to-end.
229	.. NOTE: Submission awaits terminal state inline. The two iterations of the loop run serially because the await blocks the loop body before the next submission; this is load-bearing. An earlier non-awaiting variant caused the late-clearing-probe-collision wedge — the airgap probe queued for ~19 minutes behind subsequent gauntlet dispatches, and when it finally cleared, the next tether conjure (e.g., `ordain_conjure_jupyter`) wedged in `QUEUED` until `queueTtl=3600s` expired the build. See `Memos/memo-20260517-cloudbuild-default-quota-wedge/`. The serialized await also makes the airgap probe's slot acquisition fast in the no-contention case, since no other gauntlet builds are competing during the levy window.
230	.. NOTE: In-build assertion failure (e.g., airgap pool reaching the public internet) surfaces in the build's terminal status as a hard infra-drift signal — operators inspect via the console link in the probe block.
231	.. NOTE: The `rbi_df` namespace is a flat sibling of `rbi_hm` / `rbi_rq` / `rbi_es` under the GAR repository. {rbtgo_image_audit} / {rbtgo_image_rekon} enumerators ignore it by design — probe artifacts are operational, not part of the hallmark / reliquary / enshrine image catalogue. Filenames under `rbi_df` form a flat sea (no subdirs); each filename names one depot-scoped OCI artifact.

//axhos_step
. *Submit Per-Pool Probe Builds*:
.. For each worker pool variant in [`tether`, `airgap`]:
... {rbbc_submit}
link:https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.builds/create[builds.create] with:
.... `serviceAccount`: `projects/«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»/serviceAccounts/«MASON_EMAIL»`
.... `options.pool.name`: full pool resource path for the variant
.... `options.logging`: `CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY`
.... `steps[0]`: egress-posture assertion (builder: `gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk:alpine`)
..... tether asserts `https://example.com` reachable AND `https://storage.googleapis.com` reachable
..... airgap asserts `https://example.com` blocked AND `https://storage.googleapis.com` reachable
.... `steps[1]`: GAR write probe (builder: `gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker`) — `FROM scratch` image with a single marker file, pushed to `«REGION»-docker.pkg.dev/«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»/«GAR_REPOSITORY»/rbi_df/probe-«VARIANT»:probe`
... {rbbc_require}
submission returns HTTP 200/201 (build accepted) OR HTTP 400 (per-pool quota row absent — see below). All other codes are fatal.
... {rbbc_await}
(HTTP 200/201 only) build terminal state via `builds.get` polled every 5s, with a 480-poll (40-minute) ceiling. Any terminal state — SUCCESS / FAILURE / CANCELLED / TIMEOUT / EXPIRED / INTERNAL_ERROR — is acceptable; the probe is designed to FAILURE for egress posture assertion. The HTTP 400 branch has no build to await.
... {rbbc_show}
per-pool probe block carrying the pool ID, GAR artifact reference, and either the build ID + Cloud Build console link + terminal status (HTTP 200/201) or a quota-row-materialized notice (HTTP 400).
.. NOTE: Builder images are Google-hosted only (`gcr.io/...`). The airgap pool cannot reach `docker.io`; any non-Google builder would hang the build.
.. NOTE: On fresh levy the pool's per-resource concurrent-build quota row does not yet exist. Cloud Build's quota gate refuses the submission with HTTP 400, and that refusal IS the materialization side-effect that creates the quota row — not a failure to surface. The operator can subsequently request a quota increase against the now-existing row, then re-run levy (or any subsequent build submission) to exercise the probe end-to-end.
.. NOTE: Submission awaits terminal state inline. The two iterations of the loop run serially because the await blocks the loop body before the next submission; this is load-bearing. An earlier non-awaiting variant caused the late-clearing-probe-collision wedge — the airgap probe queued for ~19 minutes behind subsequent gauntlet dispatches, and when it finally cleared, the next tether conjure (e.g., `ordain_conjure_jupyter`) wedged in `QUEUED` until `queueTtl=3600s` expired the build. See `Memos/memo-20260517-cloudbuild-default-quota-wedge/`. The serialized await also makes the airgap probe's slot acquisition fast in the no-contention case, since no other gauntlet builds are competing during the levy window.
.. NOTE: In-build assertion failure (e.g., airgap pool reaching the public internet) surfaces in the build's terminal status as a hard infra-drift signal — operators inspect via the console link in the probe block.
.. NOTE: The `rbi_df` namespace is a flat sibling of `rbi_hm` / `rbi_rq` / `rbi_es` under the GAR repository. {rbtgo_image_audit} / {rbtgo_image_rekon} enumerators ignore it by design — probe artifacts are operational, not part of the hallmark / reliquary / enshrine image catalogue. Filenames under `rbi_df` form a flat sea (no subdirs); each filename names one depot-scoped OCI artifact.
